prevalence.population: Brazilian population (highest risk in Amazon region)
prevalence.trend: stable to decreasing nationally; recurrent outbreaks in Amazon municipalities

incidence.value: ~0.3 per 1,000/year nationally (much higher in endemic municipalities)

burden.mortalityRate: Low; clustered severe cases in remote areas

vulnerablePopulations: Amazon residents (Acre, Amazonas, Pará, Rondônia, Roraima, Amapá)
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Thick/thin smear microscopy or rapid diagnostic test; species identification; follow national malaria guidelines

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no population screening; testing of febrile cases in endemic areas via local posts and reference labs
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: High cure rates when treated early; access gaps in remote riverine/indigenous areas

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: SUS endemic-area fever protocols with smear microscopy and RDTs; surveillance via SIVEP-Malária
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Chloroquine + primaquine for P. vivax; artemisinin-based combination therapy for P. falciparum per Ministério da Saúde; G6PD considerations for primaquine
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Geographic barriers in Amazon (travel time to diagnostics), outbreaks linked to mining and deforestation, and adherence challenges for primaquine
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Established endemic surveillance (SIVEP-Malária) and standardized SUS treatment protocols in the Amazon network
